en.extract:
The king vulture (Sarcoramphus papa) is a large bird found in Central and South America. It is a member of the New World vulture family Cathartidae. This vulture lives predominantly in tropical lowland forests stretching from southern Mexico to northern Argentina. It is the only surviving member of the genus Sarcoramphus, although fossil members are known.
Large and predominantly white, the king vulture has gray to black ruff, flight, and tail feathers. The head and neck are bald, with the skin color varying, including yellow, orange, blue, purple, and red. The king vulture has a noticeable orange fleshy caruncle on its beak. This vulture is a scavenger and it often makes the initial cut into a fresh carcass. It also displaces smaller New World vulture species from a carcass. King vultures have been known to live for up to 30 years in captivity.
King vultures were popular figures in the Mayan codices as well as in local folklore and medicine. Although currently listed as Least Concern by the IUCN, they are decreasing in number, due primarily to habitat loss.


Etymology, taxonomy, and systematics
The king vulture was originally described by Carl Linnaeus in 1758 in the tenth edition of his Systema Naturae as Vultur papa, the type specimen originally collected in Suriname. It was reassigned to the genus Sarcoramphus in 1805 by French zoologist André Marie Constant Duméril. The generic name is a Neo-Latin compound formed from the Greek words σάρξ (sarx, "flesh", the combining form of which is σαρκο-) and ῥάμφος (rhamphos, "crooked beak of bird of prey"). The genus name is often misspelled as Sarcorhamphus, improperly retaining the Greek rough breathing despite agglutination with the previous word-element. The bird was also assigned to the genus Gyparchus by Constantin Wilhelm Lambert Gloger in 1841, but this classification is not used in modern literature since Sarcoramphus has priority as the earlier name. The species name is derived from the Latin word papa "bishop", alluding to the bird's plumage resembling the clothing of one. The king vulture's closest living relative is the Andean condor, Vultur gryphus. Some authors have even put these species in a separate subfamily from the other New World vultures, though most authors consider this subdivision unnecessary.
There are two theories on how the king vulture earned the "king" part of its common name. The first is that the name is a reference to its habit of displacing smaller vultures from a carcass and eating its fill while they wait. An alternative theory reports that the name is derived from Mayan legends, in which the bird was a king who served as a messenger between humans and the gods. This bird was also known as the "white crow" by the Spanish in Paraguay. It was called cozcacuauhtli in Nahuatl, derived from cozcatl "collar" and cuauhtli "bird of prey".
The exact systematic placement of the king vulture and the remaining six species of New World vultures remains unclear. Though both are similar in appearance and have similar ecological roles, the New World and Old World vultures evolved from different ancestors in different parts of the world. Just how different the two are is currently under debate, with some earlier authorities suggesting that the New World vultures are more closely related to storks. More recent authorities maintain their overall position in the order Falconiformes along with the Old World vultures or place them in their own order, Cathartiformes. The South American Classification Committee has removed the New World vultures from Ciconiiformes and instead placed them in Incertae sedis, but notes that a move to Falconiformes or Cathartiformes is possible. Like other New World vultures, the king vulture has a diploid chromosome number of 80.


Fossil record and evolution

The genus Sarcoramphus, which today contains only the king vulture, had a wider distribution in the past. The Kern vulture (Sarcoramphus kernense), lived in southwestern North America during the mid-Pliocene (Piacenzian), some 3.5–2.5 million years ago). It was a little-known component of the Blancan/Delmontian faunal stages. The only material is a broken distal humerus fossil, found at Pozo Creek, Kern County, California. As per Loye H. Miller's original description, "[c]ompared with [S. papa] the type conforms in general form and curvature except for its greater size and robustness." The large span in time between the existence of the two species suggests that the Kern vulture might be distinct, but as the fossil is somewhat damaged and rather non-diagnostic, even assignment to this genus is not completely certain. During the Late Pleistocene, another species probably assignable to the genus, Sarcoramphus fisheri, occurred in Peru. A supposed king vulture relative from Quaternary cave deposits in Cuba turned out to be bones of the eagle-sized hawk Buteogallus borrasi (formerly in Titanohierax).
Little can be said of the evolutionary history of the genus, mainly because remains of other Neogene New World vultures are usually younger or even more fragmentary. The teratorns held sway over the ecological niche of the extant group, especially in North America. The Kern vulture seems to slightly precede the main bout of the Great American Interchange, and notably the living diversity of New World vultures seems to have originated in Central America. The Kern vulture would therefore represent a northwards divergence possibly sister to the S. fisheri – S. papa lineage. The fossil record, though scant, supports the theory that the ancestral king vultures and South American condors separated at least some 5 mya.


Bartram's "painted vulture"
 
A "painted vulture" ("Sarcoramphus sacra" or "S. papa sacra") is described in William Bartram's notes of his travels in Florida during the 1770s. This bird's description matches the appearance of the king vulture except that it had a white, not black, tail. Bartram describes the bird as being relatively common and even claimed to have collected one. However, no other naturalists recorded the painted vulture in Florida and 60 years after the sighting, its validity began to be questioned, leading to what John Cassin described as the most inviting problem in North American ornithology. An independent account and a painting was made of a similar bird by Eleazar Albin in 1734.
While most early ornithologists defended Bartram's honesty, Joel Asaph Allen argued that the painted vulture was mythical and that Bartram mixed elements of different species to create this bird. Allen pointed out that the birds' behavior, as recorded by Bartram, is in complete agreement with the caracara's. For example, Bartram observed the birds following wildfires to scavenge for burned insects and box turtles. Such behavior is typical of caracaras, but the larger and shorter-legged king vultures are not well adapted for walking. The crested caracara (Caracara plancus) was believed to be common and conspicuous in Bartram's days, but it is notably absent from Bartram's notes if the painted vulture is accepted as a Sarcoramphus. However, Francis Harper argued that the bird could, as in the 1930s, have been rare in the area Bartram visited and could have been missed.
Harper noticed that Bartram's notes were considerably altered and expanded in the printed edition, and the detail of the white tail appeared in print for the first time in this revised account. Harper believed that Bartram could have tried to fill in details of the bird from memory and got the tail coloration wrong. Harper and several other researchers have attempted to prove the former existence of the king vulture, or a close relative, in Florida at this late date, suggesting that the population was in the process of extinction and finally disappeared during a cold spell. Additionally, William McAtee, noting the tendency of birds to form Floridian subspecies, suggested that the white tail could be a sign that the painted vulture was a subspecies of the king vulture.


Description

Excluding the two species of condors, the king vulture is the largest of the New World vultures. Its overall length ranges from 67 to 81 cm (26–32 in) and its wingspan is 1.2 to 2 m (4–7 ft). Its weight ranges from 2.7 to 4.5 kg (6–10 lb). An imposing bird, the adult king vulture has predominantly white plumage, which has a slight rose-yellow tinge to it. In stark contrast, the wing coverts, flight feathers, and tail are dark grey to black, as is the prominent thick neck ruff. The head and neck are devoid of feathers, the skin shades of red and purple on the head, vivid orange on the neck, and yellow on the throat. On the head, the skin is wrinkled and folded, and there is a highly noticeable irregular golden crest attached to the cere above its orange and black bill; this caruncle does not fully form until the bird's fourth year.
The king vulture has, relative to its size, the largest skull and braincase, and strongest bill, of the New World vultures. This bill has a hooked tip and a sharp cutting edge. The bird has broad wings and a short, broad, and square tail. The irises of its eyes are white and bordered by bright red sclera. Unlike some New World vultures, the king vulture lacks eyelashes. It also has gray legs and long, thick claws.

The vulture is minimally sexually dimorphic, with no difference in plumage and little in size between males and females. The juvenile vulture has a dark bill and eyes, and a downy, gray neck that soon begins to turn the orange of an adult. Younger vultures are a slate gray overall, and, while they look similar to the adult by the third year, they do not completely molt into adult plumage until they are around five or six years of age. Jack Eitniear of the Center for the Study of Tropical Birds in San Antonio, Texas reviewed the plumage of birds in captivity of various ages and found that ventral feathers were the first to begin turning white from two years of age onwards, followed by wing feathers, until the full adult plumage was achieved. The final immature stages being a scattered black feathers in the otherwise white lesser wing coverts.
The vulture's head and neck are featherless as an adaptation for hygiene, though there are black bristles on parts of the head; this lack of feathers prevents bacteria from the carrion it eats from ruining its feathers and exposes the skin to the sterilizing effects of the sun.
Dark-plumaged immature birds may be confused with turkey vultures, but soar with flat wings, while the pale-plumaged adults could feasibly be confused with the wood stork, although the latter's long neck and legs allow for easy recognition from afar.


Distribution and habitat
The king vulture inhabits an estimated 14 million square kilometres (5,400,000 sq mi) between southern Mexico and northern Argentina. In South America, it does not live west of the Andes, except in western Ecuador, north-western Colombia and far north-western Venezuela. It primarily inhabits undisturbed tropical lowland forests as well as savannas and grasslands with these forests nearby. It is often seen near swamps or marshy places in the forests. This bird is often the most numerous or only vulture present in primary lowland forests in its range, but in the Amazon rainforest, it is typically outnumbered by the greater yellow-headed vulture, while typically outnumbered by the lesser yellow-headed, turkey, and American black vulture in more open habitats. King vultures generally do not live above 1,500 m (5,000 ft), although are found in places at 2,500 m (8,000 ft) altitude east of the Andes, and have been rarely recorded up to 3,300 m (11,000 ft) They inhabit the emergent forest level, or above the canopy. Pleistocene remains have been recovered from Buenos Aires Province in central Argentina, over 700 km (450 miles) south of its current range, giving rise to speculation on the habitat there at the time which had not been thought to be suitable.


Ecology and behavior
The king vulture soars for hours effortlessly, only flapping its wings infrequently. While in flight, its wings are held flat with slightly raised tips, and from a distance the vulture can appear to be headless while in flight. Its wing beats are deep and strong. Birds have been observed engaging in tandem flight on two occasions in Venezuela by naturalist Marsha Schlee, who has proposed it could be a part of courtship behaviour.Despite its size and gaudy coloration, this vulture is quite inconspicuous when it is perched in trees. While perched, it holds its head lowered and thrust forward. It is non-migratory and, unlike the turkey, lesser yellow-headed and American black vulture, it generally lives alone or in small family groups. Groups of up to 12 birds have been observed bathing and drinking in a pool above a waterfall in Belize. One or two birds generally descend to feed on a carcass, although occasionally up to ten or so may gather if there is a significant amount of food. King vultures have lived up to 30 years in captivity, though a male transferred from the Sacramento Zoo to the Queens Zoo is over 47 years old. Vivian, a female king vulture at Cameron Park Zoo in Waco, Texas, turned 70 years old in 2022 and is the oldest known king vulture in an AZA-accredited facility. Their lifespan in the wild is unknown. This vulture uses urohidrosis, defecating on its legs, to lower its body temperature. Despite its bill and large size, it is relatively unaggressive at a kill. The king vulture lacks a voice box, although it can make low croaking noises and wheezing sounds in courtship, and bill-snapping noises when threatened. Its only natural predators are snakes, which will prey upon the vulture's eggs and young, and large cats such as jaguars, which may surprise and kill an adult vulture at a carcass.


Breeding

The reproductive behaviour of the king vulture in the wild is poorly known, and much knowledge has been gained from observing birds in captivity, particularly at the Paris Menagerie. 
An adult king vulture sexually matures when it is about four or five years old, with females maturing slightly earlier than males. The birds mainly breed during the dry season. A king vulture mates for life and generally lays a single unmarked white egg in its nest in a hollow in a tree. To ward off potential predators, the vultures keep their nests foul-smelling. Both parents incubate the egg for 52 to 58 days before it hatches. If the egg is lost, it will often be replaced after about six weeks. The parents share incubating and brooding duties until the chick is about a week old, after which they often stand guard rather than brood. The young are semi-altricial—they are helpless when born but are covered in downy feathers (truly altricial birds are born naked), and their eyes are open at birth. Developing quickly, the chicks are fully alert by their second day, able to beg and wriggle around the nest, preen themselves, and peck by their third day. They start growing their second coat of white down by day 10 and stand on their toes by day 20. From one to three months of age, chicks walk around and explore the vicinity of the nest, and take their first flights at about three months of age.


Feeding

The king vulture eats anything from cattle carcasses down to corpses of monkeys and other arboreal mammals to beached fish and dead lizards.  In densely forested areas, mammals likely
to be included are many of the abundant sloths (Choloepus/Bradypus) whose combined ranges coincide largely with that of this vulture, but elsewhere it has adapted well to domestic livestock. Principally a carrion eater, there are isolated reports of it killing and eating injured animals, newborn calves, and small lizards. Although it locates food by vision, the role smell has in how it specifically finds carrion has been debated. Consensus has been that it does not detect odours, and instead follows the smaller turkey and greater yellow-headed vultures, which do have a sense of smell, to a carcass, but a 1991 study demonstrated that the king vulture could find carrion in the forest without the aid of other vultures, suggesting that it locates food using an olfactory sense. The king vulture primarily eats carrion found in the forest, though it is known to venture onto nearby savannas in search of food. Once it has found a carcass, the king vulture displaces the other vultures because of its large size and strong bill. However, when it is at the same kill as the larger Andean condor, the king vulture always defers to it. Using its bill to tear, it makes the initial cut in a fresh carcass. This allows the smaller, weaker-beaked vultures, which can not open the hide of a carcass, access to the carcass after the king vulture has fed. The vulture's tongue is rasp-like, which allows it to pull flesh off of the carcass's bones. Generally, it only eats the skin and harder parts of the tissue of its meal. The king vulture has also been recorded eating fallen fruit of the moriche palm when carrion is scarce in Bolívar state, Venezuela.


Conservation

This bird is a species of least concern to the IUCN, with an estimated range of 14 million square kilometres (5,400,000 sq mi) and between 10,000 and 100,000 wild individuals. However, there is evidence that suggests a decline in population, though it is not significant enough to cause it to be listed. This decline is due primarily to habitat destruction and poaching. Although distinctive, its habit of perching in tall trees and flying at high altitudes renders it difficult to monitor.


Relationship with humans

The king vulture is one of the most common species of birds represented in the Maya codices. Its glyph is easily distinguishable by the knob on the bird's beak and by the concentric circles that make up the bird's eyes. Sometimes the bird is portrayed as a god with a human body and a bird head. According to Maya mythology, this god often carried messages between humans and the other gods. It may also be used to represent Cozcacuauhtli, the thirteenth day of the month in the Aztec calendar (13 Reed). An ocellated turkey (Meleagris ocellata) was also considered to be the bird depicted, but the hooked bill and wattle point to the raptor.
The bird's blood and feathers were also used to cure diseases. The king vulture is also a popular subject on the stamps of the countries within its range. It appeared on a stamp for El Salvador in 1963, Belize in 1978, Guatemala in 1979, Honduras in 1997, Bolivia in 1998, and Nicaragua in 1999.
Because of its large size and beauty, the king vulture is an attraction at zoos around the world. The king vulture is one of several bird species with an AZA studbook, which is kept by Shelly Collinsworth of the Fort Worth Zoo.


References


External links

The '''king vulture''' ('''''Sarcoramphus papa''''') is a large [[bird]] found in [[Central America|Central]] and [[South America]]. It is a member of the [[New World vulture]] [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Cathartidae]]. This vulture lives predominantly in tropical lowland forests stretching from southern [[Mexico]] to northern [[Argentina]]. It is the only surviving member of the genus ''[[Sarcoramphus]]'', although fossil members are known.

Large and predominantly white, the king vulture has gray to black ruff, flight, and tail [[feather]]s. The head and neck are bald, with the skin color varying, including yellow, orange, blue, purple, and red. The king vulture has a noticeable orange fleshy [[Caruncle (bird anatomy)|caruncle]] on its beak. This vulture is a scavenger and it often makes the initial cut into a fresh carcass. It also displaces smaller New World vulture species from a carcass. King vultures have been known to live for up to 30 years in captivity.

King vultures were popular figures in the [[Maya codices|Mayan codices]] as well as in local folklore and medicine. Although currently listed as [[Least Concern]] by the [[IUCN]], they are decreasing in number, due primarily to [[habitat loss]].

== Etymology, taxonomy, and systematics ==
The king vulture was originally described by [[Carl Linnaeus]] in 1758 in the [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|tenth edition]] of his ''[[Systema Naturae]]'' as ''Vultur papa'',<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=C | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | title=Systema naturae per regna tria naturae, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis. Tomus I. Editio decima, reformata. | publisher=Holmiae. (Laurentii Salvii). | year=1758| page=86|quote= V. naribus carunculatis, vertice colloque denudate|language=la| title-link=Systema naturae }}</ref> the type specimen originally collected in [[Suriname]].<ref name=HBW>{{cite book|author=Houston, D.C.|chapter=Family Cathartidae (New World vultures)|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0001unse/page/24 24–41]|editor1=del Hoyo, Josep|editor2=Elliott, Andrew|editor3=Sargatal, Jordi (')|title=Volume 2: New World Vultures to Guineafowl of Handbook of the Birds of the World|year=1994|isbn=84-87334-15-6|publisher=Lynx edicions|location=Barcelona|chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0001unse/page/24}}</ref> It was reassigned to the genus ''Sarcoramphus'' in 1805 by French zoologist [[André Marie Constant Duméril]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Duméril | first=A. M. Constant | author-link=André Marie Constant Duméril | date=1805 | title=Zoologie analytique: ou, Méthode naturelle de classification des animaux; endue plus facile a l'aide de tableaux synoptiques  | language=French | location=Paris | publisher=Allais | page=32 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/12052583 }} The book bears the date of 1806 on the title page but was actually published in 1805. See: {{cite journal| last=Gregory | first=Steven M.S. | year=2010 | title=The two 'editions' of Duméril's ''Zoologie analytique'', and the potential confusion caused by Froriep's translation ''Analytische Zoologie'' | journal=Zoological Bibliography | volume=1 | issue=1 | pages=6–8 | url=https://iucn-tftsg.org/wp-content/uploads/file/Articles/Gregory_2010.pdf }}</ref> The generic name is a [[Neo-Latin]] compound formed from the [[Ancient Greek|Greek]] words {{Lang|grc|σάρξ}} ({{Lang|grc-Latn|sarx}}, "flesh", the combining form of which is {{Lang|grc|σαρκο-}}) and {{Lang|grc|ῥάμφος}} ({{Lang|grc-Latn|rhamphos}}, "crooked beak of bird of prey").<ref>{{cite book|last=Liddell|first=Henry George|author2=Robert Scott|year=1980|title=Greek-English Lexicon, Abridged Edition|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=Oxford|isbn=0-19-910207-4|url=https://archive.org/details/lexicon00lidd}}</ref> The genus name is often misspelled as ''Sarcor'''h'''amphus'', improperly retaining the Greek [[rough breathing]] despite [[agglutination]] with the previous word-element. The bird was also assigned to the genus ''Gyparchus'' by [[Constantin Wilhelm Lambert Gloger]] in 1841, but this classification is not used in modern literature since ''Sarcoramphus'' has priority as the earlier name.<ref>{{cite web| last = Peterson| first = Alan P. | title = Richmond Index – Genera Aaptus – Zygodactylus | work = The Richmond Index| publisher = Division of Birds at the National Museum of Natural History| date = 23 December 2007| url = http://www.zoonomen.net/cit/RI/Genera/RIGenAZ.html| access-date =17 January 2008}}</ref> The species name is derived from the [[Latin]] word {{Lang|la|papa}} "bishop", alluding to the bird's plumage resembling the clothing of one.<ref name="Likoff">{{cite book|author=Likoff, Laurie|title=The Encyclopedia of Birds|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ILEMaU74oXgC&pg=PA560|year=2007|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-0-8160-5904-1|pages=557–60|chapter=King Vulture}}</ref> The king vulture's closest living relative is the [[Andean condor]], ''Vultur gryphus''.<ref name=" taxonomy ">{{Cite journal| author = Amadon, Dean|author-link=Dean Amadon |title = Notes on the Taxonomy of Vultures| journal = [[The Condor (journal)|The Condor]]| volume = 79 | issue =4| pages = 413–16| year = 1977 | url =http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v079n04/p0413-p0416.pdf |access-date=13 March 2009| doi = 10.2307/1367720| jstor = 1367720| publisher = The Condor, Vol. 79, No. 4}}</ref> Some authors have even put these species in a separate subfamily from the other New World vultures, though most authors consider this subdivision unnecessary.<ref name="taxonomy"/>

There are two theories on how the king vulture earned the "king" part of its common name. The first is that the name is a reference to its habit of displacing smaller vultures from a carcass and eating its fill while they wait.<ref>{{cite book|author=Wood, John George |title=The illustrated natural history |url=https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_BEwDAAAAQAAJ|year=1862|publisher=Routledge, Warne and Routledge|location=London|pages=[https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_BEwDAAAAQAAJ/page/n26 15]–17}}</ref> An alternative theory reports that the name is derived from [[Maya civilization|Mayan]] legends, in which the bird was a king who served as a messenger between humans and the gods.<ref name=" NGeo">{{cite web | title =King Vulture | publisher = National Geographic | url = http://animals.nationalgeographic.com/animals/birds/king-vulture.html | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070703130015/http://animals.nationalgeographic.com/animals/birds/king-vulture.html | archive-date = July 3, 2007 | access-date = 11 September 2007}}</ref> This bird was also known as the "white crow" by the [[Spain|Spanish]] in [[Paraguay]].<ref name=" Wood">{{cite book | last = Wood| first =John George | title =The illustrated natural history | publisher =Oxford University| year= 1862| url =https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_BEwDAAAAQAAJ| isbn = 0-19-913383-2}}</ref> It was called {{Lang|nah|cozcacuauhtli}} in [[Nahuatl]], derived from {{Lang|nah|cozcatl}} "collar" and {{Lang|nah|cuauhtli}} "bird of prey".<ref name="meso">{{cite book|author1=Diehl, Richard A. |author2=Berlo, Janet Catherine |title=Mesoamerica after the decline of Teotihuacan, A.D. 700–900, Parts 700–900|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FLJxLlGkpiUC&pg=PA36|isbn=0-88402-175-0|pages=36–37|year=1989|publisher=Dumbarton Oaks }}</ref>

The exact [[systematics|systematic]] placement of the king vulture and the remaining six species of [[New World vulture]]s remains unclear.<ref name="SACC"/> Though both are similar in appearance and have similar [[Ecological niche|ecological roles]], the New World and [[Old World vultures]] evolved from different ancestors in different parts of the world. Just how different the two are is currently under debate, with some earlier authorities suggesting that the New World vultures are more closely related to [[storks]].<ref>{{cite book|author=Sibley, Charles G.|author-link=Charles Sibley|author2=Monroe, Burt L..|year=1990|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ISBN0300049692|title=Distribution and Taxonomy of the Birds of the World|publisher=Yale University Press|isbn=0-300-04969-2|access-date=11 April 2007}}{{Dead link|date=October 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> More recent authorities maintain their overall position in the order [[Falconiformes]] along with the Old World vultures<ref>[[Charles Sibley|Sibley, Charles G.]], and [[Jon Edward Ahlquist|Ahlquist, Jon E.]] 1991. ''[https://books.google.com/books?vid=ISBN0300040857 Phylogeny and Classification of Birds: A Study in Molecular Evolution]''. Yale University Press. {{ISBN|0-300-04085-7}}. Retrieved 11 April 2007.</ref> or place them in their own order, Cathartiformes.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Ericson | first1 = Per G. P. | last2 = Anderson | first2 = Cajsa L. | last3 = Britton | first3 = Tom | last4 = Elżanowski | first4 = Andrzej | last5 = Johansson | first5 = Ulf S. | last6 = Kallersjö | first6 = Mari | last7 = Ohlson | first7 = Jan I. | last8 = Parsons | first8 = Thomas J. | last9 = Zuccon | first9 = Dario | last10 = Mayr | first10 = Gerald | year = 2006 | title = Diversification of Neoaves: integration of molecular sequence data and fossils | url = http://www.systbot.uu.se/staff/c_anderson/pdf/neoaves.pdf | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20061108091145/http://www.systbot.uu.se/staff/c_anderson/pdf/neoaves.pdf | archive-date = 2006-11-08 | journal = [[Biology Letters]] | volume =  2| issue = 4| pages =  543–547| doi = 10.1098/rsbl.2006.0523 | pmid = 17148284 | pmc = 1834003 }}</ref> The [[South American Classification Committee]] has removed the New World vultures from [[Ciconiiformes]] and instead placed them in ''[[Incertae sedis]]'', but notes that a move to Falconiformes or Cathartiformes is possible.<ref name="SACC">Remsen, J. V. Jr.; C. D. Cadena; A. Jaramillo; M. Nores; J. F. Pacheco; M. B. Robbins; T. S. Schulenberg; F. G. Stiles; D. F. Stotz & K. J. Zimmer. 2007. [http://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.html ''A classification of the bird species of South America.''] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090302073659/http://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.html |date=2009-03-02 }} South American Classification Committee. Retrieved on 15 October 2007</ref> Like other New World vultures, the king vulture has a diploid chromosome number of 80.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Chromosomal analysis in Cathartidae: distribution of heterochromatic blocks and rDNA, and phylogenetic considerations|author=Tagliarini, Marcella Mergulhão|author2=Pieczarka, Julio Cesar|author3=Nagamachi, Cleusa Yoshiko|author4=Rissino, Jorge|author5=de Oliveira, Edivaldo Herculano C. |year=2009|journal=Genetica|volume=135|issue=3|pages=299–304|doi=10.1007/s10709-008-9278-2|pmid=18504528|s2cid=22786201}}</ref>

=== Fossil record and evolution ===
[[File:Sarcoramphus papa Natural History Museum University of Pisa AV 7098.jpg|thumb|''Sarcoramphus papa'' skeleton]]
The genus ''Sarcoramphus'', which today contains only the king vulture, had a wider distribution in the past. The Kern vulture (''Sarcoramphus kernense''), lived in southwestern [[North America]] during the mid-[[Pliocene]] ([[Piacenzian]]), some 3.5–2.5 million years ago). It was a little-known component of the [[Blancan]]/Delmontian [[faunal stage]]s. The only material is a broken [[Anatomical terms of location#Proximal and distal|distal]] [[humerus]] [[fossil]], found at Pozo Creek, [[Kern County, California]]. As per [[Loye H. Miller]]'s original description, "[c]ompared with [''S. papa''] the [[type (biology)|type]] conforms in general form and curvature except for its greater size and robustness."<ref name = miller1931/> The large span in time between the existence of the two species suggests that the Kern vulture might be distinct, but as the fossil is somewhat damaged and rather non-diagnostic, even assignment to this genus is not completely certain.<ref name=Fisher44/> During the [[Late Pleistocene]], another species probably assignable to the genus, ''Sarcoramphus fisheri'', occurred in [[Peru]].<ref>{{cite book | last = Wilbur | first = Sanford | title = Vulture Biology and Management | publisher = University of California Press | location = Berkeley | year = 1983 |page=12| isbn = 0-520-04755-9 }}</ref> A supposed king vulture relative from [[Quaternary]] cave deposits in [[Cuba]] turned out to be bones of the [[eagle]]-sized [[hawk]] ''[[Buteogallus borrasi]]'' (formerly in ''Titanohierax'').<ref name=suarez/>

Little can be said of the [[evolution]]ary history of the genus, mainly because remains of other [[Neogene]] New World vultures are usually younger or even more fragmentary. The [[teratorn]]s held sway over the [[ecological niche]] of the extant group, especially in North America. The Kern vulture seems to slightly precede the main bout of the [[Great American Interchange]], and notably the living diversity of New World vultures seems to have originated in [[Central America]].<ref name = miller1931 /> The Kern vulture would therefore represent a northwards divergence possibly sister to the ''S. fisheri – S. papa'' lineage. The fossil record, though scant, supports the theory that the ancestral king vultures and South American condors separated at least some 5 [[mya (unit)|mya]].<ref>{{cite book | last = Wilbur | first = Sanford | title = Vulture Biology and Management | publisher = University of California Press | location = Berkeley | year = 1983 |page=6| isbn = 0-520-04755-9 }}</ref>

=== Bartram's "painted vulture" ===
[[File:Sarcorhamphus sacer Albin.jpg|thumb|upright|[[Eleazar Albin]]'s drawing from 1734, which has sometimes been identified as the "painted vulture"]] 
A "'''painted vulture'''" ("'''''Sarcoramphus sacra'''''" or "'''''S. papa sacra'''''") is described in [[William Bartram]]'s notes of his travels in [[Florida]] during the 1770s. This bird's description matches the appearance of the king vulture except that it had a white, not black, tail.<ref name="Harper">{{Cite journal | last = Harper | first = Francis | author-link = Francis Harper (biologist) | title = The ''Vutlur sacra'' of William Bartram | journal = Auk | volume = 53 | issue = 4 | pages = 381–392 | publisher = American Ornithologist's Union | location = Lancaster, PA | date = October 1936 | url = http://extinct-website.co.uk/pdf/p0381-p0392.pdf | access-date = 2010-11-15 | doi = 10.2307/4078256 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303172854/http://extinct-website.co.uk/pdf/p0381-p0392.pdf | archive-date = 2016-03-03 | jstor = 4078256 }}</ref> Bartram describes the bird as being relatively common and even claimed to have collected one.<ref name="Harper"/> However, no other naturalists recorded the painted vulture in Florida and 60 years after the sighting, its validity began to be questioned, leading to what [[John Cassin]] described as the most inviting problem in North American ornithology.<ref name="Harper"/> An independent account and a painting was made of a similar bird by [[Eleazar Albin]] in 1734.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Snyder | first1 = N. F. R.  | last2 = Fry | first2 = J. T.  | year = 2013 | title = Validity of Bartram's Painted Vulture | journal = Zootaxa | volume = 3613 | issue = 1 | pages =  61–82| doi=10.11646/zootaxa.3613.1.3 | pmid = 24698902 | s2cid = 5536272 }}</ref>

While most early ornithologists defended Bartram's honesty, [[Joel Asaph Allen]] argued that the painted vulture was mythical and that Bartram mixed elements of different species to create this bird.<ref name="Harper"/> Allen pointed out that the birds' behavior, as recorded by Bartram, is in complete agreement with the [[Caracara (subfamily)|caracara]]'s.<ref name="Harper"/> For example, Bartram observed the birds following [[wildfire]]s to scavenge for burned insects and [[box turtle]]s. Such behavior is typical of caracaras, but the larger and shorter-legged king vultures are not well adapted for walking. The [[crested caracara]] (''Caracara plancus'') was believed to be common and conspicuous in Bartram's days, but it is notably absent from Bartram's notes if the painted vulture is accepted as a ''Sarcoramphus''.<ref name="Harper"/> However, [[Francis Harper (biologist)|Francis Harper]] argued that the bird could, as in the 1930s, have been rare in the area Bartram visited and could have been missed.<ref name="Harper"/>

Harper noticed that Bartram's notes were considerably altered and expanded in the printed edition, and the detail of the white tail appeared in print for the first time in this revised account. Harper believed that Bartram could have tried to fill in details of the bird from memory and got the tail coloration wrong.<ref name="Harper"/> Harper and several other researchers have attempted to prove the former existence of the king vulture, or a close relative, in Florida at this late date, suggesting that the population was in the process of [[extinct]]ion and finally disappeared during a cold spell.<ref name="Harper"/><ref name=" Day">Day, David (1981): ''The Doomsday Book of Animals'': Ebury, London/Viking, New York. {{ISBN|0-670-27987-0}}</ref> Additionally, William McAtee, noting the tendency of birds to form Floridian subspecies, suggested that the white tail could be a sign that the painted vulture was a subspecies of the king vulture.<ref>{{Cite journal | last = McAtee | first = William Lee | title = Bartram's Painted Vulture | journal = Auk | volume = 59 | issue = 1 | page = 104 | publisher = American Ornithologist's Union | location = Lancaster, PA | date = January 1942 | url = http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v059n01/p0104-p0104.pdf| access-date = 15 November 2010 | doi = 10.2307/4079172| jstor = 4079172 }}</ref>

== Description ==
{{Multiple image
| image1            = Sarcoramphus papa (Königsgeier - King Vulture) - Weltvogelpark Walsrode 2013-01.jpg
| align             = right
| image2            = King Vulture.jpg
| total_width       = 400
| footer            = Side views of head, showing the distinctive colorful head and beak
}}
[[File:King vultures.jpg|thumb|Juvenile and adult in [[Costa Rica]]]]
Excluding the two species of [[condor]]s, the king vulture is the largest of the New World vultures. Its overall length ranges from {{convert|67|to(-)|81|cm|in|0|abbr=on}} and its wingspan is {{convert|1.2|to(-)|2|m|ft|0|abbr=on}}. Its weight ranges from {{convert|2.7|to(-)|4.5|kg|lb|0|abbr=on}}.<ref name="NGeo"/><ref name=RotW>{{cite book|author1=Ferguson-Lees, James |author2=Christie, David A |title=Raptors of the World|pages=88, 315–16|publisher=Houghton Mifflin Company|year=2001|isbn=978-0-618-12762-7|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hlIztc05HTQC&pg=PA88}}</ref> An imposing bird, the adult king vulture has predominantly white plumage, which has a slight rose-yellow tinge to it.<ref name=" Howell ">{{Cite book| last1 = Howell| first1 = Steve N.G.| last2 = Webb| first2 = Sophie| title = A Guide to the Birds of Mexico and Northern Central America| place = New York| publisher = Oxford University Press| year = 1995| page = [https://archive.org/details/guidetobirdsofme0000howe/page/176 176]| isbn = 0-19-854012-4| title-link = A Guide to the Birds of Mexico and Northern Central America}}</ref> In stark contrast, the wing coverts, flight feathers, and tail are dark grey to black, as is the prominent thick neck ruff.<ref name=HBW/> The head and neck are devoid of feathers, the skin shades of red and purple on the head, vivid orange on the neck, and yellow on the throat.<ref name="Terres">{{cite book| last =Terres| first =J. K.| author-link =John Kenneth Terres| title =The Audubon Society Encyclopedia of North American Birds| publisher =Knopf| year =1980| location =New York, NY| page =[https://archive.org/details/audubonsocietyen00terr/page/959 959]| isbn =0-394-46651-9| url =https://archive.org/details/audubonsocietyen00terr/page/959}}</ref> On the head, the skin is wrinkled and folded, and there is a highly noticeable irregular golden crest attached to the [[cere]] above its orange and black bill;<ref name=HBW/> this [[Caruncle (bird anatomy)|caruncle]] does not fully form until the bird's fourth year.<ref name=" Gyparchus ">{{cite book | last = Gurney| first =John Henry | title =A descriptive catalogue of the raptorial birds in the Norfolk and Norwich museum| publisher =Oxford University| year= 1864| url =https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_REwDAAAAQAAJ| access-date =13 March 2009}}</ref>

The king vulture has, relative to its size, the largest skull and braincase, and strongest bill, of the New World vultures.<ref name=Fisher44/> This bill has a hooked tip and a sharp cutting edge.<ref name="Likoff"/> The bird has broad wings and a short, broad, and square tail.<ref name=" Howell "/> The [[Iris (anatomy)|irises]] of its eyes are white and bordered by bright red [[sclera]].<ref name=HBW/> Unlike some New World vultures, the king vulture lacks eyelashes.<ref name=" Pterylosis ">{{Cite journal| last = Fisher| first = Harvey I.| title = The Pterylosis of the King Vulture| journal = Condor| volume = 45 | issue = 2| pages = 69–73| date= March 1943| url = http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v045n02/p0069-p0073.pdf| access-date = 13 March 2009| doi = 10.2307/1364380| jstor = 1364380| publisher = The Condor, Vol. 45, No. 2}}</ref> It also has gray legs and long, thick claws.<ref name=" Howell "/>
[[File:King Vulture soaring at Pacifico Lodge (cropped).jpg|thumb|In flight]]
The vulture is minimally [[sexual dimorphism|sexually dimorphic]], with no difference in plumage and little in size between males and females.<ref name="HBW" /> The juvenile vulture has a dark bill and eyes, and a downy, gray neck that soon begins to turn the orange of an adult. Younger vultures are a slate gray overall, and, while they look similar to the adult by the third year, they do not completely molt into adult plumage until they are around five or six years of age.<ref name="Howell" /> Jack Eitniear of the Center for the Study of Tropical Birds in San Antonio, Texas reviewed the plumage of birds in captivity of various ages and found that ventral feathers were the first to begin turning white from two years of age onwards, followed by wing feathers, until the full adult plumage was achieved. The final immature stages being a scattered black feathers in the otherwise white lesser wing coverts.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Estimating age classes in king vultures (''Sarcoramphus papa'') using plumage coloration |author=Eitniear, Jack Clinton|journal=Journal of Raptor Research|volume=30|issue=1|pages=35–38|year=1996|url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jrr/v030n01/p00035-p00038.pdf |access-date=16 November 2010}}</ref>

The vulture's head and neck are featherless as an adaptation for hygiene, though there are black bristles on parts of the head; this lack of feathers prevents [[bacteria]] from the [[carrion]] it eats from ruining its feathers and exposes the skin to the sterilizing effects of the sun.<ref name="Likoff" /><ref name="Who">{{cite web | title =Sarcoramphus papa | publisher =Who Zoo | url =http://whozoo.org/Anlife99/scottmen/newvulture.htm | access-date =11 September 2007 | archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20140901152245/http://www.whozoo.org/Anlife99/scottmen/newvulture.htm | archive-date =1 September 2014 }}</ref>

Dark-plumaged immature birds may be confused with turkey vultures, but soar with flat wings, while the pale-plumaged adults could feasibly be confused with the [[wood stork]],<ref name="Hilty">{{cite book|author1=Hilty, Stephen L. |author2=Brown, William L. |author3=Brown, Bill | title =A guide to the birds of Colombia| publisher =Princeton University Press| year= 1986| location =Princeton, NJ| page = 88| isbn = 0-691-08372-X|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kHa6tJNKGDAC&pg=PA88}}</ref> although the latter's long neck and legs allow for easy recognition from afar.<ref name="Henderson">{{cite book|author1=Henderson, Carrol L. |author2=Adams, Steve |author3=Skutch, Alexander F. | title =Birds of Costa Rica: A Field Guide| publisher =University of Texas Press| year= 2010| page = 66| isbn = 978-0-292-71965-1|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=TxpqRO8dB8AC&pg=PA66}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==

The king vulture inhabits an estimated {{convert|14|e6km2|sqmi|-5}} between southern [[Mexico]] and northern [[Argentina]].<ref name="BirdLife">{{cite web | title = Species factsheet: Sarcoramphus papa|year=2010 | publisher =[[BirdLife International]] | url = http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/index.html?action=SpcHTMDetails.asp&sid=3823&m=0 | access-date =11 September 2007}}</ref> In [[South America]], it does not live west of the [[Andes]],<ref name="Gyparchus" /> except in western [[Ecuador]],<ref>{{cite book | last1 = Ridgely|first1 = Robert|last2 = Greenfield| first2 = Paul| title = Birds of Ecuador: Field Guide| page = 74| year = 2001| publisher = Cornell University Press|isbn=0-8014-8721-8}}</ref> north-western [[Colombia]] and far north-western [[Venezuela]].<ref>{{cite book | last1 = Restall| first1 = Robin| last2 = Rodner| first2 = Clemencia| last3 = Lentino| first3 = Miguel| title = Birds of Northern South America: An Identification Guide. Vol. 2| page = 68| year = 2006| publisher = Christopher Helm | isbn=0-7136-7243-9}}</ref> It primarily inhabits undisturbed [[Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests|tropical lowland forests]] as well as [[savanna]]s and [[grassland]]s with these forests nearby.<ref>{{cite book |last=Brown |first=Leslie |title=Birds of Prey: Their biology and ecology |page=59|year=1976 |publisher=Hamlyn |isbn=0-600-31306-9}}</ref> It is often seen near [[swamp]]s or marshy places in the forests.<ref name="Wood" /> This bird is often the most numerous or only vulture present in primary lowland forests in its range, but in the [[Amazon rainforest]], it is typically outnumbered by the [[greater yellow-headed vulture]], while typically outnumbered by the [[lesser yellow-headed vulture|lesser yellow-headed]], [[turkey vulture|turkey]], and [[American black vulture]] in more open habitats.<ref>{{cite book | last1 = Restall| first1 = Robin| last2 = Rodner| first2 = Clemencia| last3 = Lentino| first3 = Miguel| title=Birds of Northern South America: An Identification Guide. Vol. 1 | pages=80–83 | year=2006 | publisher= Christopher Helm | isbn=0-7136-7242-0}}</ref> King vultures generally do not live above {{convert|1500|m|ft|-3|abbr=on}}, although are found in places at {{convert|2500|m|ft|-3|abbr=on}} altitude east of the Andes, and have been rarely recorded up to {{convert|3300|m|ft|-3|abbr=on}}<ref name="RotW" /> They inhabit the emergent forest level, or above the canopy.<ref name="HBW" /> Pleistocene remains have been recovered from [[Buenos Aires Province]] in central Argentina, over {{convert|700|km|mi|round=50|abbr=in}} south of its current range, giving rise to speculation on the habitat there at the time which had not been thought to be suitable.<ref>{{cite journal|title=First record of ''Sarcoramphu''s Dumeril 1806 (Ciconiiformes: Vulturidae) from the Pleistocene of Buenos Aires province, Argentina |author1=Noriega, Jorge I. |author2=Areta, Juan I. |journal=Journal of South American Earth Sciences |volume=20 |issue=1–2 (SI) |pages=73–79 |year=2005 |url=http://www.grupofalco.com.ar/pedefes/Noriega_Areta_sarcoramhpus.pdf |access-date=10 November 2010 |doi=10.1016/j.jsames.2005.05.004 |bibcode=2005JSAES..20...73N |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110706081435/http://www.grupofalco.com.ar/pedefes/Noriega_Areta_sarcoramhpus.pdf |archive-date= 6 July 2011 }}</ref>

== Ecology and behavior ==
The king vulture soars for hours effortlessly, only flapping its wings infrequently.<ref name="Henderson" /><ref name="Schulenberg">{{Cite book|author=Schulenberg, Thomas S. | title =Birds of Peru| page=84|publisher=Princeton University Press |location= Princeton, NJ|year=2007|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yFuWUc7l0uQC&pg=PA82|isbn=978-0-691-13023-1}}</ref> While in flight, its wings are held flat with slightly raised tips, and from a distance the vulture can appear to be headless while in flight.<ref name="Ridgely">{{Cite book|author1=Ridgely, Robert S. |author2=Gwynne, John A. Jr. | title = A Guide to the Birds of Panama with Costa Rica, Nicaragua, and Honduras| location= Princeton, NJ| publisher = Princeton University Press| year =1989| edition = Second| page = 84| isbn =0-691-02512-6}}</ref> Its wing beats are deep and strong.<ref name="Howell" /> Birds have been observed engaging in tandem flight on two occasions in Venezuela by naturalist Marsha Schlee, who has proposed it could be a part of courtship behaviour.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Schlee, Marsha|title=First record of tandem flying in the King Vulture (''Sarcoramphus papa'')|journal=Journal of Raptor Research| volume=35|issue=3|pages=263–64|year= 2001|url=http://64.106.42.23/sora/jrr/v035n03/p00263-p00264.pdf|access-date=16 November 2010}}</ref>[[File:Sarcoramphus-papa-king-vulture-closeup-0a.jpg|right|thumb|Adult at the [[Berlin Zoo]]]]Despite its size and gaudy coloration, this vulture is quite inconspicuous when it is perched in trees.<ref name="Ridgely" /> While perched, it holds its head lowered and thrust forward.<ref name="RotW" /> It is [[bird migration|non-migratory]] and, unlike the [[turkey vulture|turkey]], [[lesser yellow-headed vulture|lesser yellow-headed]] and [[American black vulture]], it generally lives alone or in small family groups.<ref name="Eureka">{{cite web| last =Bellinger| first = Jack| title = King Vulture AZA Studbook| date= March 25, 1997 | url = http://www.eurekawebs.com/zoo/kv.htm | access-date = 8 October 2007|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20061110111820/http://www.eurekawebs.com/zoo/kv.htm |archive-date = November 10, 2006}}</ref> Groups of up to 12 birds have been observed bathing and drinking in a pool above a waterfall in Belize.<ref>{{cite journal|journal=Journal of Raptor Research| title=Observations of king vultures (''Sarcoramphus papa'') drinking and bathing|author1=Baker, Aaron J. |author2=Whitacre, David F. |author3=Aguirre, Oscar |volume=30|issue=4|pages=246–47|year=1996}}</ref> One or two birds generally descend to feed on a carcass, although occasionally up to ten or so may gather if there is a significant amount of food.<ref name="HBW" /> King vultures have lived up to 30 years in captivity, though a male transferred from the Sacramento Zoo to the Queens Zoo is over 47 years old. Vivian, a female king vulture at Cameron Park Zoo in Waco, Texas, turned 70 years old in 2022 and is the oldest known king vulture in an AZA-accredited facility.<ref>{{Cite web |title=King Vulture {{!}} Cameron Park Zoo |url=https://www.cameronparkzoo.com/explore/animals/birds/king-vulture/ |access-date=2022-08-02 |language=en-US |archive-date=2022-08-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220802182039/https://www.cameronparkzoo.com/explore/animals/birds/king-vulture/ }}</ref> Their lifespan in the wild is unknown.<ref name="NGeo" /> This vulture uses [[urohidrosis]], defecating on its legs, to lower its body temperature. Despite its bill and large size, it is relatively unaggressive at a kill.<ref name="HBW" /> The king vulture lacks a [[Syrinx (biology)|voice box]], although it can make low croaking noises and wheezing sounds in courtship, and bill-snapping noises when threatened.<ref name="RotW" /> Its only natural predators are [[snake]]s, which will prey upon the vulture's eggs and young, and large cats such as [[jaguar]]s, which may surprise and kill an adult vulture at a carcass.<ref name="ADW">{{cite web | title = Sarcoramphus papa | author=Ormiston, D. | publisher = Animal Diversity Web | url = http://animaldiversity.ummz.umich.edu/site/accounts/information/Sarcoramphus_papa.html| access-date = 11 September 2007}}</ref>

=== Breeding ===
[[File:Brevard Zoo, Australian Section - Flickr - Rusty Clark (12).jpg|left|thumb|Egg exhibited at [[Brevard Zoo]]]]
The reproductive behaviour of the king vulture in the wild is poorly known, and much knowledge has been gained from observing birds in captivity,<ref name="deroy98">{{cite journal|author=de Roy, Tui|year=1998|title=King of the Jungle|journal=International Wildlife|issue=28|pages=52–57|issn=0020-9112}}</ref> particularly at the [[Ménagerie du Jardin des Plantes|Paris Menagerie]].<ref name="schlee94">{{cite journal|author=Schlee, Marsha A. |year=1994|title=Reproductive Biology in King Vultures at the Paris Menagerie|journal=International Zoo Yearbook|issue=1|pages=159–75|doi=10.1111/j.1748-1090.1994.tb03570.x|volume=33}}</ref> 

An adult king vulture sexually matures when it is about four or five years old, with females maturing slightly earlier than males.<ref name="Grady">{{Cite book | last = Grady | first = Wayne | title = Vulture: Nature's Ghastly Gourmet | publisher = Sierra Club Books | year = 1997 | location = San Francisco | page = [https://archive.org/details/vulturenaturesgh00grad/page/47 47] | isbn = 0-87156-982-5 | url = https://archive.org/details/vulturenaturesgh00grad/page/47 }}</ref> The birds mainly breed during the dry season.<ref name="ADW" /> A king vulture mates for life and generally lays a single unmarked white [[egg (biology)|egg]] in its nest in a hollow in a tree.<ref name="Howell" /> To ward off potential predators, the vultures keep their nests foul-smelling.<ref name="deroy98" /> Both parents incubate the egg for 52 to 58 days before it hatches. If the egg is lost, it will often be replaced after about six weeks. The parents share incubating and brooding duties until the chick is about a week old, after which they often stand guard rather than brood. The young are semi-[[altricial]]—they are helpless when born but are covered in downy feathers (truly altricial birds are born naked), and their eyes are open at birth. Developing quickly, the chicks are fully alert by their second day, able to beg and wriggle around the nest, preen themselves, and peck by their third day. They start growing their second coat of white down by day 10 and stand on their toes by day 20. From one to three months of age, chicks walk around and explore the vicinity of the nest, and take their first flights at about three months of age.<ref name="schlee94" /><!-- cites previous six sentences -->

=== Feeding===
[[File:King vulture and black vultures feeding.jpg|thumb|Feeding, with [[Black vulture|black vultures]]]]
The king vulture eats anything from cattle carcasses down to corpses of monkeys and other arboreal mammals to beached fish and dead lizards.<ref name="Ferguson-Lees, J. 2001">Ferguson-Lees, J. & Christie, D.A. & Franklin, K. & Mead, D. & Burton, P.. (2001). Raptors of the world. Helm Identification Guides.</ref>  In densely forested areas, mammals likely
to be included are many of the abundant sloths (''[[Choloepus]]''/''[[Bradypus]]'') whose combined ranges coincide largely with that of this vulture, but elsewhere it has adapted well to domestic livestock.<ref name="Ferguson-Lees, J. 2001" /> Principally a carrion eater, there are isolated reports of it killing and eating injured animals, newborn calves, and small lizards.<ref name="RotW" /><!-- cites both previous sentences --> Although it locates food by vision, the role smell has in how it specifically finds carrion has been debated. Consensus has been that it does not detect odours, and instead follows the smaller [[turkey vulture|turkey]] and [[greater yellow-headed vulture]]s, which do have a sense of smell, to a carcass,<ref name="HBW" /><ref name="Strike">{{cite journal| author =Beason, Robert C.| title = Through a Birds Eye: Exploring Avian Sensory Perception|journal= Bird Strike Committee USA/Canada, 5th Joint Annual Meeting, Toronto, ONT|publisher =University of Nebraska| year= 2003| url =http://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1007&context=birdstrike2003| format = PDF| access-date = 11 September 2007}}</ref> but a 1991 study demonstrated that the king vulture could find carrion in the forest without the aid of other vultures, suggesting that it locates food using an [[Olfaction|olfactory]] sense.<ref name="Foraging">{{Cite journal| last = Lemon| first = William C| title = Foraging behavior of a guild of Neotropical vultures| journal = Wilson Bulletin| volume = 103| issue = 4| pages = 698–702| date= December 1991| url = http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v103n04/p0698-p0702.pdf| access-date=13 March 2009}}</ref> The king vulture primarily eats carrion found in the forest, though it is known to venture onto nearby savannas in search of food. Once it has found a carcass, the king vulture displaces the other vultures because of its large size and strong bill.<ref name="HBW" /> However, when it is at the same kill as the larger [[Andean condor]], the king vulture always defers to it.<ref name="EoC">{{cite web| title = Ecology of Condors| url =http://www.ecology.info/condors.htm| archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20061001235133/http://www.ecology.info/condors.htm| archive-date =1 October 2006| access-date = 5 October 2006}}</ref> Using its bill to tear, it makes the initial cut in a fresh carcass. This allows the smaller, weaker-beaked vultures, which can not open the hide of a carcass, access to the carcass after the king vulture has fed.<ref name="HBW" /><!-- cites two previous sentences --> The vulture's tongue is [[rasp]]-like, which allows it to pull flesh off of the carcass's bones.<ref name="Who" /> Generally, it only eats the skin and harder parts of the tissue of its meal.<ref name="ADW" /> The king vulture has also been recorded eating fallen fruit of the [[moriche palm]] when carrion is scarce in [[Bolívar (state)|Bolívar state]], Venezuela.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Schlee, Marsha|title=King vultures (''Sarcoramphus papa'') forage in moriche and cucurit palm stands|journal=Journal of Raptor Research| volume=39|issue=4|pages=458–61|year= 2005|url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jrr/v039n04/p00458-p00461.pdf|access-date=10 November 2010}}</ref>

== Conservation ==
[[File:Sarcorhanphus papa.jpg|right|thumb|[[London Zoo]], 2006]]
This bird is a species of [[least concern]] to the [[IUCN]],<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /> with an estimated range of {{convert|14|e6km2|sqmi|-5}} and between 10,000 and 100,000 wild individuals. However, there is evidence that suggests a decline in population, though it is not significant enough to cause it to be listed.<ref name="BirdLife"/> This decline is due primarily to [[habitat destruction]] and [[poaching]].<ref name=" Eureka "/> Although distinctive, its habit of perching in tall trees and flying at high altitudes renders it difficult to monitor.<ref name=HBW/>

== Relationship with humans ==
[[File:Signo Cozcacuauhtli.png|right|thumb|100px|Cozcacuauhtli from the [[Codex Laud]]]]
The king vulture is one of the most common species of birds represented in the [[Maya codices]].<ref name=" Maya">{{cite book | last = Tozzer| first =Alfred Marston |author2=Glover Morrill Allen | title =Animal Figures in the Maya Codices
 | publisher =Harvard University| year= 1910| url =https://archive.org/details/animalfiguresin00allegoog}}</ref> Its [[glyph]] is easily distinguishable by the knob on the bird's beak and by the concentric circles that make up the bird's eyes.<ref name=" Maya" /> Sometimes the bird is portrayed as a god with a human body and a bird head.<ref name=" Maya"/> According to Maya mythology, this god often carried messages between humans and the other gods.<ref name="ADW"/> It may also be used to represent Cozcacuauhtli, the thirteenth day of the month in the [[Aztec calendar]] (13 Reed). An [[ocellated turkey]] (''Meleagris ocellata'') was also considered to be the bird depicted, but the hooked bill and wattle point to the raptor.<ref name="meso"/>

The bird's blood and feathers were also used to cure diseases.<ref name="Who"/> The king vulture is also a popular subject on the stamps of the countries within its range. It appeared on a stamp for [[El Salvador]] in 1963, [[Belize]] in 1978, [[Guatemala]] in 1979, [[Honduras]] in 1997, [[Bolivia]] in 1998, and [[Nicaragua]] in 1999.<ref name="Stamp ">{{cite web | title =King Vulture | publisher =Bird Stamps | url = http://www.bird-stamps.org/cspecies/2800700.htm | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20001117183700/http://www.bird-stamps.org/cspecies/2800700.htm | url-status = usurped | archive-date = November 17, 2000 | access-date = 17 October 2007}}</ref>

Because of its large size and beauty, the king vulture is an attraction at zoos around the world. The king vulture is one of several bird species with an [[Association of Zoos and Aquariums|AZA]] studbook, which is kept by Shelly Collinsworth of the [[Fort Worth Zoo]].<ref name=" AZA ">{{cite web|title=Vulture, King Studbook |work=AZA website |year=2010 |publisher=Association of Zoos and Aquariums |location=Silver Spring, MD |url=http://www.aza.org/AnimalCare/AnimalPrograms/detail.aspx?id=4197 |access-date=15 November 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110904202212/http://www.aza.org/AnimalCare/AnimalPrograms/detail.aspx?id=4197 |archive-date= 4 September 2011 }} ''(subscription required)''</ref>
